title: Cat Open Wound Oozing Yellow Pus: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn what to do if your cat has an infected wound with yellow pus. Understand why this high-urgency situation requires immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
What is a Purulent Cat Wound?

When you see yellow or creamy discharge oozing from a cat's wound, it is medically referred to as purulent exudate. This is a clear indicator of a bacterial infection, often caused by a bite or a puncture wound that has formed an abscess. As your cat's body tries to fight the bacteria, white blood cells and debris accumulate, resulting in the pus you see.

Urgency Level: High

An open wound with yellow pus is considered a high-priority situation. Infections can spread rapidly through a cat's bloodstream, potentially leading to a life-threatening condition called sepsis. If your cat is also lethargic, hiding, or refusing food, they need to be seen by a veterinarian or an emergency clinic immediately.

The Role of Triage Photos

Providing a high-quality photo to your veterinary team or a telehealth professional can significantly speed up the triage process. A clear image allows the clinical staff to evaluate the size of the opening, the extent of surrounding tissue inflammation, and the severity of the infection. This visual information helps them determine if your cat needs to be rushed into surgery or if they can be stabilized in an exam room first.

faqs.question: Can I treat an infected cat wound at home?
faqs.answer: No, an infected wound with pus requires professional debridement and prescription antibiotics. Home remedies or over-the-counter ointments often trap bacteria inside, which can lead to a much deeper and more dangerous infection.

faqs.question: What if the wound has a foul smell?
faqs.answer: A foul odor often indicates tissue death (necrosis) or a specific type of anaerobic bacteria. This increases the urgency for veterinary intervention, as the infection is likely advanced.

faqs.question: Should I bandage the wound before going to the vet?
faqs.answer: It is generally best to leave the wound open unless there is excessive bleeding. Bandaging an infected, oozing wound can trap heat and bacteria, worsening the infection. Keep the cat in a clean, confined area until you can reach the clinic.
